revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽承昭|}} (September 7, 1840-July 19, 1916) was a Japanese [[daimyo]] of the [[Edo period]], who ruled the [[Hirosaki Domain]]. His court title was ''[[Kokushi (official)|Tosa no kami]]''. Under his leadership, the Hirosaki domain reorganized and modernized its military force. In the Boshin War of 1868-69, these modernized Hirosaki forces took part in the final assault on the [[Republic of Ezo]].
Tsuguakira was created [[count]] in 1884.
==References== ''This article is derived from [http://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B4%A5%E8%BB%BD%E6%89%BF%E6%98%AD corresponding content] on the Japanese wikipedia.
{{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Lord of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=[[1859]]-[[1871]]}} {{end box}}

His court title was ''[[Kokushi (official)|Tosa no kami]]''. Under his leadership, the Hirosaki domain reorganized and modernized its military force. In the Boshin War of 1868-69, these modernized Hirosaki forces took part in the final assault on the [[Republic of Ezo]].
Tsuguakira was created [[count]] in 1884.

revision.text:
{{Underconstruction}} {{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽承昭|}} (September 7, 1840-July 19, 1916) was a Japanese [[daimyo]] of the [[Edo period]], who ruled the [[Hirosaki Domain]]. His court title was ''[[Kokushi (official)|Tosa no kami]]''. Under his leadership, the Hirosaki domain reorganized and modernized its military force. In the Boshin War of 1868-69, these modernized Hirosaki forces took part in the final assault on the [[Republic of Ezo]]. He was created [[count]] in 1884.
==Early Life and Adoption== ==Career as Lord== ==Actions in the Boshin War== {{Main|Boshin War}} ==Later Life== Tsuguakira was created [[count]] in 1884. He later worked as a supervisor in the Number 15 National Bank (第十五国立銀行, Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō?).
==References== *''This article is derived from [http://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B4%A5%E8%BB%BD%E6%89%BF%E6%98%AD corresponding content] on the Japanese wikipedia. *''300 han hanshu jinmei jiten'' vol. 1., pp. 138-139.

revision.comment: expand, cleanup format
revision.text:
{{Infobox person |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira
津軽承昭 |image = |alt = |caption = | birth_date={{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} | birth_place=[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | death_date={{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan |nationality = Japanese |other_names = |known_for = |occupation = Daimyō of Hirosaki Domain (1859-1871) }} {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 - July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His courtesy title was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]].'' ==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''daimyō'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He was married to the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859, and continued his predecessor’s policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]], and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].McClellan, p. 175.Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153. However, the Tsugaru soon switched course, and briefly joined the [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]].Onodera, p. 140. However, for reasons yet unclear, the Tsugaru backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles in the Imperial cause during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], Yukitsugu was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taito, Tokyo|Taito-ku]], Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] Konoe Tadafusa, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872-1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People in Meiji period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[ko:쓰가루 쓰구아키라]] [[ja:津軽承昭]]

He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taito, Tokyo|Taito-ku]], Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] Konoe Tadafusa, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872-1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People in Meiji period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[ko:쓰가루 쓰구아키라]] [[ja:津軽承昭]]

Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{Persondata | NAME =Tsugaru Tsuguakira | ALTERNATIVE NAMES = | SHORT DESCRIPTION = | DATE OF BIRTH =September 7, 1840 | PLACE OF BIRTH =[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | DATE OF DEATH =July 19, 1916 | PLACE OF DEATH =[[Tokyo]], Japan }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People in Meiji period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[ko:쓰가루 쓰구아키라]] [[ja:津軽承昭]]

revision.text:
{{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{Infobox person |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira
津軽承昭 |image = |alt = |caption = | birth_date={{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} | birth_place=[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | death_date={{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan |nationality = Japanese |other_names = |known_for = |occupation = Daimyō of Hirosaki Domain (1859-1871) }} {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His courtesy title was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]].'' ==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''daimyō'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He was married to the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859, and continued his predecessor’s policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]], and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].McClellan, p. 175.Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153. However, the Tsugaru soon switched course, and briefly joined the [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]].Onodera, p. 140. However, for reasons yet unclear, the Tsugaru backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles in the Imperial cause during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], Yukitsugu was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taito, Tokyo|Taito-ku]], Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] Konoe Tadafusa, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872-1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{Persondata | NAME =Tsugaru Tsuguakira | ALTERNATIVE NAMES = | SHORT DESCRIPTION = | DATE OF BIRTH =September 7, 1840 | PLACE OF BIRTH =[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | DATE OF DEATH =July 19, 1916 | PLACE OF DEATH =[[Tokyo]], Japan }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People in Meiji period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[ko:쓰가루 쓰구아키라]] [[ja:津軽承昭]]

He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taito, Tokyo|Taito-ku]], Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872-1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{Persondata | NAME =Tsugaru Tsuguakira | ALTERNATIVE NAMES = | SHORT DESCRIPTION = Japanese Daimyo | DATE OF BIRTH =September 7, 1840 | PLACE OF BIRTH =[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | DATE OF DEATH =July 19, 1916 | PLACE OF DEATH =[[Tokyo]], Japan }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People of Meiji-period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]]

revision.text:
{{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{Infobox person |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira
津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |alt = |caption = Tsugaru Tsuguakira (1913) | birth_date={{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} | birth_place=[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | death_date={{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan |nationality = Japanese |other_names = |known_for = |occupation = Daimyō of Hirosaki Domain (1859-1871) }} {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His courtesy title was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]].'' ==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''daimyō'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He was married to the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859, and continued his predecessor’s policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]], and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].McClellan, p. 175.Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153. However, the Tsugaru soon switched course, and briefly joined the [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]].Onodera, p. 140. However, for reasons yet unclear, the Tsugaru backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles in the Imperial cause during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], Yukitsugu was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taito, Tokyo|Taito-ku]], Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872-1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{Persondata | NAME =Tsugaru Tsuguakira | ALTERNATIVE NAMES = | SHORT DESCRIPTION = Japanese Daimyo | DATE OF BIRTH =September 7, 1840 | PLACE OF BIRTH =[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | DATE OF DEATH =July 19, 1916 | PLACE OF DEATH =[[Tokyo]], Japan }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People of Meiji-period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]]

After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|25px]] 12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People of Meiji-period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[Category:Japanese bankers]]

{{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{Infobox person |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira
津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |alt = |caption = Tsugaru Tsuguakira (1913) | birth_date={{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} | birth_place=[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | death_date={{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan |nationality = Japanese |other_names = | predecessor = [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | successor = -none- | spouse = daughter of Tsugaru Yukitsugu | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] | children = | mother = | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] |known_for = |occupation = ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] (1859–1871) }} '''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His courtesy title was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and the title of ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles |Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. ==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He was married to the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].McClellan, p. 175.Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153. The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],Onodera, p. 140. but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], Tsuguakira was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{ja icon}} [http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|25px]] 12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People of Meiji-period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[Category:Japanese bankers]]

{{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{Infobox person |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira
津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |alt = |caption = Tsugaru Tsuguakira (1913) | birth_date={{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} | birth_place=[[Edo]], [[Japan]] | death_date={{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan |nationality = Japanese |other_names = | predecessor = [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | successor = -none- | spouse = daughter of Tsugaru Yukitsugu | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] | children = | mother = | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] |known_for = |occupation = ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] (1859–1871) }} '''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His courtesy title was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and the title of ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles |Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. ==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He was married to the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857. Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008) first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].McClellan, p. 175.Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153. The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],Onodera, p. 140. but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], Tsuguakira was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in [[Tokyo]] in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo. Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir. ==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]] == References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha ==External links== *{{ja icon}} [http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife]{{dead link|date=August 2018|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} *{{ja icon}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{ja icon}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] ==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.'' {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|25px]] 12th [[Hirosaki Domain|Daimyō of Hirosaki]] | before=[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | after=none | years=1859-1871}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsugaru, Tsuguakira}} [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:Tsugaru clan]] [[Category:Higo-Hosokawa clan]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1916 deaths]] [[Category:People of the Boshin War]] [[Category:People of Meiji-period Japan]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[Category:Japanese bankers]]

revision.text:
{{Japanese name|Tsugaru}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= [[Count]] |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= [[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|20px]] 12th ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 =[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] |successor1 = < position abolished > |office2= Imperial Governor of Kuroishi |monarch2 = [[Emperor Meiji]] |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = [[Edo]], [[Japan]] |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles |Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] <ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML'']
==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.''

==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]] <ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML'']

(Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha

Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}

{{family name hatnote|Tsugaru|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= [[Count]] |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= [[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|20px]] 12th ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 =[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] |successor1 = ''[[Abolition of the han system|Position abolished]]'' |office2= Imperial Governor of Hirosaki |monarch2 = [[Emperor Meiji]] |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = [[Edo]], [[Japan]] |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}
==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.''

''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha

{{family name hatnote|Tsugaru|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= [[Count]] |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= [[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|20px]] 12th ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 =[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] |successor1 = ''[[Abolition of the han system|Position abolished]]'' |office2= Imperial Governor of Hirosaki |monarch2 = [[Emperor Meiji]] |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = [[Edo]], [[Japan]] |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}

(Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]

{{family name hatnote|Tsugaru|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= [[Count]] |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= [[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|20px]] 12th ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 =[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] |successor1 = ''[[Abolition of the han system|Position abolished]]'' |office2= Imperial Governor of Hirosaki |monarch2 = [[Emperor Meiji]] |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = [[Edo]], [[Japan]] |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}
==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.''

==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}

{{family name hatnote|Tsugaru|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= [[Count]] |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= [[file:Japanese crest Tugaru Botann.svg|20px]] 12th ''[[Daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 =[[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] |successor1 = ''[[Abolition of the han system|Position abolished]]'' |office2= Imperial Governor of Hirosaki |monarch2 = [[Emperor Meiji]] |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = [[Edo]], [[Japan]] |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = [[Tokyo]], Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]] | partner = daughter of [[Konoe Tadahiro]] }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}
==Notes== {{reflist}} * ''The content of much of this article was derived from that of the corresponding article on Japanese Wikipedia.''

(Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Hirosaki Domain]] in northern [[Mutsu Province]], [[Honshū]], [[Japan]] (modern-day [[Aomori Prefecture]]). His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Uto Domain]], a sub-domain of [[Kumamoto Domain]] in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of [[Tsugaru Yukitsugu]], 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[Bakumatsu period]], during which time the [[Tsugaru clan]]<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of [[Satchō Alliance]] and attacked nearby [[Shōnai Domain]].<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa [[Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei]],<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the [[Boshin War]], notably that of the [[Battle of Noheji]], and [[Battle of Hakodate]] on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the [[abolition of the han system]], he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new [[Aomori Prefecture]].
He relocated to [[Tokyo]]. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ([[count]]). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his [[waka (poetry)|''waka'' poetry]]. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the [[Yanaka Cemetery]] in [[Taitō]]-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] [[Konoe Tadafusa]], who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *[[Tsugaru clan]]
