'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *{{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *{{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.

{{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142}}; {{ill|waka poet|WD=Q11122954}}|spouse={{ill|Tsugaru Tadako|WD=Q7137201}}|relatives={{ill|Hosokawa Yoshikuni|WD=Q11606020}}|native_name=津軽承昭}} {{family name hatnote|Tsugaru|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= {{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}} |name = Tsugaru Tsuguakira |native_name = 津軽承昭 |image = Tsuguakira Tsugaru.jpg |caption =Tsugaru Tsuguakira (in 1913) |office1= 12th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Iemochi]] *[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] }} |term_start1 = 1859 |term_end1 = 1868 |predecessor1 ={{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}} |successor1 = ''{{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Position abolished}}'' |office2= Imperial Governor of Hirosaki |monarch2 = {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} |term_start2 = 1869 |term_end2 = 1871 |predecessor2 = |successor2 = |birth_date = {{birth date|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |birth_place = {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} |death_date = {{death date and age|1916|7|19|1840|9|7|mf=y}} |death_place = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, Japan | father = [[Hosokawa Narimori]] | spouse = daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}} | partner = daughter of {{ill|Konoe Tadahiro|en|Konoe Tadahiro|WD=Q909042|lt=Konoe Tadahiro}} }}
'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *{{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}

'''Count''' {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Tsuguakira'''|津軽 承昭||extra= September 7, 1840 – July 19, 1916}} was the 12th and final ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain|1=Hirosaki Domain}} in northern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}, [[Honshu|Honshū]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} (modern-day {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}). His {{ill|Courtesy title|en|Courtesy title|WD=Q2304081|lt=Courtesy title|1=Courtesy title}} was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Tosa-no-kami]]'', which was later upgraded to ''Etchū-no-kami'' and ''Jiju'', and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
==Biography== Tsugaru Tsuguakira was the 4th son of [[Hosokawa Narimori]], the 8th ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Uto Domain|en|Uto Domain|WD=Q3034413|lt=Uto Domain|1=Uto Domain}}, a sub-domain of {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|en|Kumamoto Domain|WD=Q1194248|lt=Kumamoto Domain|1=Kumamoto Domain}} in [[Kyushu]]. He married the 4th daughter of {{ill|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|en|Tsugaru Yukitsugu|WD=Q7849987|lt=Tsugaru Yukitsugu|1=Tsugaru Yukitsugu}}, 11th ''daimyō'' of Hirosaki Domain, and was adopted as his heir in 1857.
Tsuguakira became ''daimyō'' on February 7, 1859 on the retirement of Yukitsugu, and continued his predecessor's policies of modernizing and westernizing the domain's military forces. He became ''daimyō'' during the turbulent [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]], during which time the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}}<ref>Koyasu Nobushige (1880), ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' vol. 1 (Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige), p. 25. (Accessed from [http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html |date=2010-02-11 }}, 17 July 2008)</ref> first sided with the pro-imperial forces of {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|WD=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance|1=Satchō Alliance}} and attacked nearby {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|WD=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain|1=Shōnai Domain}}.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175">McClellan, p. 175.</ref><ref>Mark Ravina (1999), ''Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan'' (California: Stanford University Press), pp. 152-153.</ref> The domain then briefly defected to the pro-Tokugawa {{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|1=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}},<ref>Onodera, p. 140.</ref> but, for reasons yet unclear, soon backed out of the alliance and re-joined the imperial cause after a few months, participating in several battles during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}, notably that of the {{ill|Battle of Noheji|en|Battle of Noheji|WD=Q2889525|lt=Battle of Noheji|1=Battle of Noheji}}, and {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|WD=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate|1=Battle of Hakodate}} on the pro-Meiji side.<ref name="McClellan, p. 175"/>
After the [[Meiji Restoration]], with the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}, he was appointed Imperial Governor of Hirosaki from 1869 to 1871, at which time the territory was absorbed into the new {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}.
He relocated to {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}. With the establishment of the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system in 1882, Tsuguakira was awarded with the title of ''hakushaku'' ({{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count|1=Count}}). After his retirement from public life, he served as a director of the {{nihongo|Number 15 National Bank|第十五国立銀行|Dai jūgo kokuritsu ginkō}}, and was noted for his {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=''waka'' poetry|1=Waka (poetry)}}. He died in Tokyo in 1916, and his grave is at the {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery|1=Yanaka Cemetery}} in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|WD=Q232641|lt=Taitō|1=Taitō}}-ku, Tokyo.
Tsugaru Tsuguakira had no son, and adopted the younger son of the [[kuge|court noble]] {{ill|Konoe Tadafusa|en|Konoe Tadafusa|WD=Q3198724|lt=Konoe Tadafusa|1=Konoe Tadafusa}}, who took the name of {{nihongo|'''Tsugaru Hidemaru'''|津軽英麿||}} (1872–1919) to be his heir.
==See also== *{{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}}
== References == * Koyasu Nobushige (1880). ''Buke kazoku meiyoden'' 武家家族名誉伝 Volume 1. Tokyo: Koyasu Nobushige. (Accessed from [https://web.archive.org/web/20100211193757/http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/index.html National Diet Library], 17 July 2008) * Kurotaki, Jūjirō (1984). ''Tsugaru-han no hanzai to keibatsu'' 津軽藩の犯罪と刑罰. Hirosaki: Hoppō shinsha. * Narita, Suegorō (1975). ''Tsugaru Tamenobu: shidan'' 津軽為信: 史談. Aomori: Tōō Nippōsha. * Tsugaru Tsuguakira Kō Den kankōkai (1976). ''Tsugaru Tsuguakira kō-den'' 津輕承昭公傳. Tokyo: Rekishi Toshosha
==External links== *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20060823011332/http://www5f.biglobe.ne.jp/~sans-culotte/topics021013.html Pictures of Tsuguakira and his wife] *{{in lang|ja}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20100526083919/http://shiro39.hp.infoseek.co.jp/touhoku/hirosaki/hirosaki.htm "Hirosaki-jō"] (17 Feb. 2008) *{{in lang|ja}} [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4k-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html "Tsugaru-han" on ''Edo 300 HTML''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160314025328/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~me4K-skri/han/mutudewa/tugaru.html |date=2016-03-14 }}
